prompt: Search X + web for the pain of filling out enterprise VENDOR SECURITY QUESTIONNAIRES (SIG, CAIQ, custom) as an AI/SaaS startup in 2026 — time cost, repetition, AI-specific questions enterprises now add. Quote founder posts. Cite.
content:
**Filling out enterprise vendor security questionnaires (SIG, CAIQ, and custom forms) remains a major pain point for AI/SaaS startups in 2026, often acting as a sales bottleneck that consumes significant engineering or security resources, delays deals, and involves heavy repetition—now compounded by new AI-specific sections.**[[1]](https://secureflo.net/ai-security-questionnaires-why-most-startups-fail/)[[2]](https://www.trustcloud.ai/security-questionnaires/startups-guide-to-security-questionnaires/)

### Time Costs and Scale
Standardized questionnaires vary in length:
- **SIG (Standardized Information Gathering)**: SIG Lite is typically ~150 questions; full/core versions range from ~800–1,000+ questions.[[3]](https://x.com/_ShantanuKul/status/2061527198063001999)[[4]](https://safe.security/resources/blog/vendor-security-questionnaire-vraq-best-practices/)
- **CAIQ (Consensus Assessments Initiative Questionnaire)**: Often 260–280+ questions (or Lite versions with 70–124).[[5]](https://www.workstreet.com/blog/caiq-vs-sig)

Custom or vendor-specific questionnaires commonly run 200–400+ questions. Without a dedicated Information Security Officer, Trust Center, or pre-built evidence library (e.g., SOC 2 report, policies, architecture diagrams), each one can take **2 weeks of an engineer’s calendar time**—or 2–6 weeks for a security analyst.[[3]](https://x.com/_ShantanuKul/status/2061527198063001999)[[6]](https://x.com/chatdomain/status/2051377108262547925)

This scales poorly for startups. One X post notes that “the security questionnaire is usually where a deal stops pretending to be real,” with teams repeating answers across dozens of prospects due to a “trust-system problem.”[[7]](https://x.com/DbsCrypto/status/2062595245821309347)

### Repetition Across Prospects
The core frustration is redundancy: the same (or very similar) questions are re-asked by every enterprise prospect. Founders and teams describe copy-pasting from prior spreadsheets or responses, which feels like “sales engineering for missing proof.”[[7]](https://x.com/DbsCrypto/status/2062595245821309347) Startups without centralized, reusable answer repositories waste time on consistency checks, evidence gathering, and revisions for each new request.[[2]](https://www.trustcloud.ai/security-questionnaires/startups-guide-to-security-questionnaires/)

This repetition slows enterprise sales cycles more than pricing in some cases, with questionnaires acting as gates that can stall or kill deals.[[8]](https://x.com/FridrickCyber/status/2017443378703802856) Common startup advice in 2026 emphasizes preparing responses *before* the first deal rather than reacting mid-sales process.[[3]](https://x.com/_ShantanuKul/status/2061527198063001999)[[9]](https://x.com/ClevsecTeam/status/2040713117093470699)

### AI-Specific Questions Enterprises Now Add
A newer layer of pain in 2026 comes from **AI-specific modules** that enterprises have added to standard questionnaires (SIG, CAIQ, and custom templates). These typically add **30–60 questions** that did not exist ~18 months prior.[[1]](https://secureflo.net/ai-security-questionnaires-why-most-startups-fail/)

Common topics include:
- Model provenance, training data lineage/rights, and bias controls.
- Defenses against prompt injection, hallucination risks, and output verification.
- Alignment with frameworks like ISO 42001 or NIST AI RMF.
- AI-specific incident response, governance, and supply-chain risks for models/agents.

One source notes that “Enterprise buyers added AI modules to their standard questionnaires... Buyers ask about model provenance, training data rights, prompt injection defenses, hallucination controls, and ISO 42001 alignment.”[[1]](https://secureflo.net/ai-security-questionnaires-why-most-startups-fail/) AI/SaaS vendors must now map these to their LLM data flows, agent behaviors, and policies—often requiring new documentation or expert input that smaller teams lack.

### Founder and Practitioner Quotes from X
- “SIG Lite is ~150 questions. SIG Full is ~800. Vendor-specific ones run 200–400. If you don’t have an Information Security Officer or a Trust Center page, every questionnaire is 2 weeks of an engineer’s calendar. Solve this before the first deal, not during it.” — Shantanu Kulkarni (@_ShantanuKul).[[3]](https://x.com/_ShantanuKul/status/2061527198063001999)
- “The security questionnaire is usually where a deal stops pretending to be real. Most teams don’t have a security-answer problem. They have a trust-system problem. Copy-pasting last quarter’s spreadsheet is just sales engineering for missing proof. When revenue depends on repeating the same answers 40 times, your trust layer is broken.” — CryptoD₿S (@DbsCrypto).[[7]](https://x.com/DbsCrypto/status/2062595245821309347)
- “Security questionnaires killing enterprise deals? SIG, CAIQ & custom reviews slow SaaS sales more than pricing.” — Fridrick Cyber Tech (@FridrickCyber).[[8]](https://x.com/FridrickCyber/status/2017443378703802856)
- Pitches for AI tools (e.g., “Trustfront is an AI security analyst that turns a 600-question SIG into 4 hours”) implicitly highlight the baseline manual burden of weeks per questionnaire.[[6]](https://x.com/chatdomain/status/2051377108262547925)

This pain has spurred a wave of AI-powered automation tools and services aimed at startups and SaaS companies, but the underlying issue of repetitive, evidence-heavy assessments—now with AI overlays—persists as a 2026 reality for teams scaling into enterprise deals. Preparation via centralized policies, Trust Centers, and SOC 2-type artifacts is repeatedly cited as the most effective mitigation.
